preFix.title: Trench composting kitchen waste directly into beds
preFix.subtitle: Burying vegetable peelings and food scraps in the soil for direct in-ground decomposition
preFix.excerpt: Trench composting buries kitchen waste directly in garden beds without a bin. The soil organisms decompose the material over six to eight weeks, releasing nutrients into the surrounding root zone. No turning, no temperature monitoring, no equipment beyond a spade. For a household with a vegetable garden, it is the lowest-effort composting method available.
preFix.sourceNotes: RHS (Royal Horticultural Society) guidance on soil enrichment and organic matter incorporation. Henry Doubleday Research Association (Garden Organic) trench composting guidance. Charles Dowding no-dig and organic matter guidance (publicly available in blog form).

content.text: Trench composting is one of the oldest organic waste methods: dig a trench in the bed, fill it with kitchen scraps, and cover with the excavated soil. Worms, bacteria, fungi, and beetles work from below over the following weeks. They pull the material down into the subsoil and release nutrients. For a vegetable garden on an annual rotation, each bed can receive one load of organic matter per season with no dedicated compost heap needed.
What can go in
Vegetable and fruit peelings, cores, and off-cuts.
Coffee grounds and loose tea leaves.
Eggshells (slow to break down but worth including for calcium).
Small amounts of cooked vegetable matter.
What to keep out
Meat, fish, and dairy: these attract rats and foxes when buried near the surface. They decompose fine in a hot heap with good coverage but not in a shallow trench.
Citrus peel in large quantities: it breaks down slowly and the oils can reduce worm activity near the burial point.
Diseased plant material from the garden: blight, clubroot, and other pathogens can persist in shallow burial and infect the next crop.
The trench method
Choose a bed that will not be planted for the next six to eight weeks. Autumn trenching benefits spring planting; spring trenching benefits summer sowing.
Dig a trench 200-300 mm deep and 200 mm wide. Place the excavated soil to one side in a continuous ridge alongside the trench.
Fill the trench to within 100 mm of the surface with kitchen waste. Layer vegetable matter with torn cardboard or dry leaves if available; the carbon addition improves the
C:N ratio
and accelerates decomposition.
Backfill with the excavated soil and tread down lightly. The filled trench should be at or just below bed level; a raised ridge attracts birds that may scratch the surface.

Mark the end of the trench with a cane. On the next kitchen-waste collection (weekly or fortnightly), extend the trench by 300 mm along the bed. This 'moving trench' method works its way down the bed over a season.
Rotation with planting

In a three-bed rotation, use one bed each year for trench composting, one for growing, and one as fallow or winter cover crop. The trench bed rests for most of the season. In the following year, plant it with hungry crops such as courgettes, squash, or brassicas to use the decomposed organic matter.

attrs.fix: Ensure the cover of soil is at least 100 mm. Place a length of chicken wire over the trench surface and weight it down with stones for the first two weeks. Remove meat and dairy from trench-composted material.
attrs.cause: Material buried too close to the surface, or the trench included meat or dairy scraps
attrs.symptom: Rats or foxes have been digging up the trench

attrs.fix: Water the trench area if dry. Large pieces decompose faster if chopped before burial. Continue monitoring; complete decomposition can take up to 12 weeks in cold weather.
attrs.cause: Dry conditions, low worm activity, or too much citrus or woody material in the mix
attrs.symptom: Material is still recognisable after eight weeks

initialFindings.message: tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three

initialFindings.snippet: Diseased plant material from the garden: blight, clubroot, and other pathogens can persist in shallow burial and infect the next crop.
